planting.note: Direct sow in fall for spring germination after cold stratification, or sow in early spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; globe sedge is common in moist to wet forested bottomlands and wet woodland margins across eastern North America.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade in woodland gardens, rain gardens, and wet bottomland settings; globe sedge is a native eastern sedge recognized most easily by its striking perigynia — the inflated, balloon-like seed sacs that swell to a distinctive round shape, giving the plant the common name bladder sedge or globe sedge. Each fruiting head contains relatively few but very large, inflated perigynia that catch the eye and make this species easy to identify among woodland sedges. The plant grows naturally in wet to moist, shaded bottomlands, seepage slopes, and forested wetland margins throughout eastern North America. It is an important component of the woodland floor layer and provides seed food for wood ducks and other wetland birds. An excellent ground layer plant for moist, shaded native woodland gardens in zones 4–9.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in moist, humus-rich, calcium-rich to neutral soil in deep shade; white bear sedge is a limestone-loving sedge of rich, moist, beech-maple-basswood forests of the eastern US Midwest. It germinates best after cold stratification.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, calcium-rich to neutral soil in deep to partial shade; white bear sedge is a distinctive native sedge of rich mesic forests over limestone, particularly common in the mixed mesophytic and beech-maple forest communities of the eastern US interior. The broad, flat leaves are unusually wide for a sedge — among the broadest of the native Carex — and have a soft, somewhat glaucous appearance. In the garden, white bear sedge excels as a ground layer plant under deciduous trees in rich, slightly alkaline to neutral soil, where its lush, wide-leaved appearance provides a different texture from the finer-leaved wood sedge cultivars. It tolerates deep shade well and is semi-evergreen in mild winters. A good companion for mayapple, trillium, and other spring ephemerals in the shade garden.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for spring germination; slender woodland sedge prefers moist to moderately dry, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in dappled to deep shade. A versatile shade sedge tolerating drier conditions than many of its relatives.

directions: Plant in moderately dry to moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to deep shade; slender woodland sedge is one of the most useful native sedges for dry to moderately moist shaded conditions, growing naturally in upland deciduous forests across much of the eastern United States. The narrow, arching leaves are bright green and delicate in texture, creating an airy, graceful effect under deciduous trees. Unlike many shade sedges that require consistent moisture, slender woodland sedge tolerates dry summer shade reasonably well once established — making it valuable for the challenging dry-shade conditions under shallow-rooted trees like maples. The drooping, finger-like spikes (suggested by the species name digitalis) are inconspicuous but produced reliably each spring. An excellent, well-behaved ground layer plant that does not spread aggressively.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in wet to moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; gynandrous sedge is characteristic of forested swamps, alder thickets, and the shaded margins of ponds and streams across eastern North America.

directions: Plant in wet to consistently moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun at the shaded margins of water features, forested wetlands, and streambanks; gynandrous sedge is a tall, robust native sedge of eastern wetland forests, forming large, graceful clumps with broad, arching leaves and long, nodding spikes that droop elegantly at maturity. The nodding spike arrangement distinguishes it from many upright-spike sedges and makes it particularly graceful in moist garden settings. It grows naturally in the shade of forested swamps and alder carr communities, providing important nesting habitat for marsh birds and seed food for wood ducks and swamp sparrows. A strong architectural plant for the margin of a naturalistic pond or rain garden in partial shade where its height and drooping flower spikes make a striking statement.

planting.note: Direct sow on the surface of wet, muck, or saturated soil in partial shade to full sun; soft rush requires light for germination — do not cover seed. Alternatively, plant divisions in spring. Grows naturally in marshes, pond edges, wet meadows, and roadside ditches throughout North America.

directions: Plant in wet to saturated, mucky, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun at the edges of ponds, streams, and rain gardens; soft rush is one of the most widely distributed and ecologically important wetland plants in the world, native throughout North America, Europe, and Asia. The cylindrical, medium-green stems are leafless — photosynthesis occurs through the stem surface — and the small, brown flower clusters emerge from the side of each stem near the tip. The stems were historically used for basket weaving, rush matting, tallow-soaked rush lights, and chair seat weaving — uses that persist in traditional crafts today. In the garden, soft rush is an essential component of constructed wetlands, bioswales, and naturalistic pond margins, providing year-round architectural interest with its vertical, cylindrical stems and supporting numerous wetland invertebrates and nesting birds.
